title: West Coast Compass IPA
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A classic dank, citrus-forward American IPA with a dry finish and firm bitterness. Built as an original clone-style formulation representative of the West Coast style.
notes: Ferment at 18-19 °C for a clean profile. Dry hop for 4 days once fermentation is nearly complete, then cold-crash before packaging. Target a firm, resinous bitterness balanced by a dry, pale malt backbone.

hops.name: Citra
hops.notes: 20 min stand at ~80 °C

hops.name: Citra
hops.notes: dry hop, days

hops.name: Cascade
hops.notes: dry hop, days

title: Cascade Trail Pale Ale
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A balanced, sessionable American Pale Ale showcasing Cascade hops over a light caramel malt base. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: An easy-drinking everyday pale. Keep fermentation clean at 18-20 °C. A short dry hop lifts the aroma without overwhelming the malt.

hops.name: Cascade
hops.notes: dry hop, days

title: Ironwood Amber Ale
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A malty American Amber Ale with toasty caramel depth and a moderate citrus hop character. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: The crystal and Munich malts give a deep copper hue and caramel backbone. Balance bitterness so the malt stays in front.

title: Blackstack Dry Stout
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A dry, roasty Irish Stout with a coffee-like bitterness and a light body from flaked barley. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Roasted barley provides the signature dry, espresso finish. Flaked barley adds body and head retention. Serve on nitro if possible.

title: Locktender American Porter
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A robust American Porter with layered chocolate and coffee notes, a firm bitterness, and a smooth roasted finish. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Chocolate and black malt provide roast without the ashiness of a stout. Ferment clean and let the malt character lead.

title: Sunfeld Hefeweizen
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A cloudy German-style Weissbier with banana and clove esters from the yeast over a soft wheat malt base. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: The character comes from the yeast, not the hops — keep bitterness low. Ferment warm (18-20 °C) to accentuate banana; cooler favours clove. A protein rest supports the classic soft mouthfeel.

title: Abbey Cellar Dubbel
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A rich Belgian Dubbel with dark fruit, caramel, and a dry, dangerously drinkable finish from dark candi syrup. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Special B and dark candi syrup drive raisin and plum notes. Ferment warm (20-24 °C) to develop Belgian yeast character. The syrup boosts gravity while keeping the body deceptively light.

title: Meadowlace Witbier
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A refreshing Belgian Witbier, hazy and light-bodied, spiced with coriander and orange peel over unmalted wheat. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Add 15 g crushed coriander and 25 g bitter orange peel in the last 5 minutes of the boil. Flaked wheat and oats give the signature cloudy, silky body. Ferment at 20 °C.

title: Silberquell Pilsner
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A crisp, dry German Pilsner with a floral-spicy noble hop character and a clean lager finish. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Use soft water and a healthy lager yeast pitch. Ferment cold (10-12 °C) and lager for 3-4 weeks near 0 °C. A single Pilsner malt base keeps it pale and delicate.

title: Hollowfield Brown Ale
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: An American Brown Ale balancing nutty, chocolate malt depth with a moderate American hop presence. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Toasty, nutty malt with just enough Cascade to keep it lively. A great all-season brown. Ferment clean at 18-20 °C.

title: Fieldhand Saison
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A dry, peppery Belgian Saison with a spicy yeast character and a highly attenuated, refreshing finish. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Saison yeast attenuates very far — expect a bone-dry finish. Start fermentation at 20 °C and let it free-rise into the high 20s °C to fully attenuate. Sugar addition lightens the body.

title: Nightmill Oatmeal Stout
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A smooth, full-bodied Oatmeal Stout with silky mouthfeel, mild roast, and notes of chocolate and coffee. Original clone-style formulation.
notes: Flaked oats deliver the signature silky texture and head retention. Keep the roast restrained and the body full. Ferment at 18-20 °C.

title: Wildflower Sky traditional dry mead
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A bone-dry traditional mead made from a blend of light wildflower honeys, fermented cool with a clean wine yeast. Ferments fully to dryness for a crisp, neutral honey-wine character — the kind of mead that disappears from the glass.
notes: Dilute honey in warm non-chlorinated water to reach the target OG before pitching; pitching into undiluted honey stalls most wine yeasts. Use a staggered nutrient regimen (SNA: DAP + Fermaid-O across three additions over the first ~7 days) to keep the yeast healthy up to ~14% potential ABV. Ferment at 16-18 °C, then bulk age 3-6 months before bottling.

fermentables.name: Wildflower honey
fermentables.notes: blend of clover and orange blossom for a neutral floral base

processSteps.name: Rack off lees
processSteps.notes: move to secondary, top up if needed

title: Orchard Stand semi-sweet mead
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A balanced semi-sweet traditional mead — same honey base as the dry version but stopped before fully attenuating, then cold-stabilized for residual honey sweetness without re-fermentation in the bottle.
notes: Cold-crash in primary once gravity is close to target, then rack and stabilize with potassium sorbate + metabisulfite to lock in residual sweetness. A cooler ferment (14-16 °C) keeps the yeast strain from over-attenuating. Bulk age 2-3 months for the honey and acidity to round out.

fermentables.name: Orange blossom honey
fermentables.notes: single-source for a clean citrus-honey character

processSteps.name: Cold crash
processSteps.notes: drop yeast out of suspension

processSteps.name: Stabilize
processSteps.notes: sorbate + sulfite to lock sweetness

title: Hedgerow blackberry melomel
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A medium-sweet fruit mead built on late-summer blackberries — fruit-forward, deeply coloured, with enough acidity to keep it from feeling heavy. The fruit goes in primary so the yeast scrubs some of the harsh tannins.
notes: Freeze-thaw the fruit before pitching to break down cell walls and improve juice yield. Pectic enzyme at pitch clears pectin haze. Rack off the fruit after ~7 days to avoid extracting bitter seed tannins. Bulk age 3 months minimum for the fruit and honey to marry.

fermentables.name: Blackberries (frozen)
fermentables.notes: freeze-thawed, lightly crushed; add in primary

yeasts.name: Lalvin 71B
yeasts.notes: 71B metabolizes malic acid slightly, softens berry acidity

processSteps.name: Primary on fruit
processSteps.notes: press/push fruit down daily; rack off fruit on day 7

title: Fireside metheglin
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A warm-spiced mead built around cinnamon, clove, and dried orange peel — a holiday-style metheglin that's bigger than a beer but lighter than a fortified wine. Spice additions are kept conservative so they read as background rather than potpourri.
notes: Add whole spices to secondary, not primary: hot alcohol extraction during primary over-steeps them and turns the mead medicinal. A 3-day spice steep in secondary followed by a rack gives a clean aromatic lift. Bulk age 4-6 months for the spice to integrate.

fermentables.notes: neutral clover; lets the spices lead

fermentables.name: Brown sugar

yeasts.notes: holds spice and honey well; flocculates cleanly

processSteps.name: Spice steep in secondary
processSteps.notes: cinnamon stick, whole clove, dried orange peel — then rack off

title: Concord Pantry kit wine
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: An easy 4 L (1 gal) country wine made from a single jug of store-bought Concord grape juice — the textbook beginner kit. Ferments dry, then backsweetened to a jammy, off-dry table-wine style.
notes: Campden 24 h before pitch to knock down wild microbes. Pectic enzyme at pitch clears the heavy pectin load from grape juice. Ferment cool (16-18 °C) for a cleaner fruit profile. Stabilize with sulfite + sorbate before backsweetening so residual sugar doesn't re-ferment in the bottle.

fermentables.name: Concord grape juice
fermentables.notes: pasteurized store-bought juice, no preservatives added

yeasts.name: Lalvin EC-1118
yeasts.notes: tolerant of high-acid grape musts; ferments fully dry

title: Applefield fresh-fruit country wine
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A dry, crisp country wine made from fresh-pressed cider apples — closer to a rough French cidre style than a sweet apple wine. Fermented cool with a low-attenuation yeast to leave a touch of natural sweetness and a clean fruit finish.
notes: Use freshly pressed, untreated cider — no sorbate, no campden, no ascorbic. Campden at crush is essential; pitch 24 h later. The yeast choice keeps the wine dry-but-not-bone-dry, with apple character intact. Cold-settle in primary to drop pectin before racking.

fermentables.notes: untreated, freshly pressed from mixed cider varieties

yeasts.notes: leaves a touch of residual sugar; enhances fruity esters

title: Hollowgate pyment
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A pyment — mead-meets-grape-wine — built on a base of red grape concentrate cut with clover honey. Ferments dry, then aged on light oak for a fortified-leaning, structured table wine that drinks somewhere between a dry red and a traditional mead.
notes: Rehydrate yeast with Go-Ferm before pitching into the high-sugar must. Use a strong-fermenting wine yeast (EC-1118) to drive the mead component fully dry. Oak cubes in secondary add gentle vanilla / tannin structure without overwhelming the grape-honey balance. Bulk age 4-6 months.

title: Soft Cloud New England IPA
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A classic New England IPA — hazy, juicy, and pillowy-soft. Built on a pale-ale grist with a heavy dose of flaked oats and flaked wheat for haze stability and mouthfeel, balanced against a soft, chloride-forward water profile that lifts the late-hop character without sharpening the bitterness.
notes: Water profile: target ~200 ppm chloride and ~60 ppm sulfate (roughly a 3:1 Cl:SO4 ratio) with calcium around 80 ppm — the chloride is what gives the beer its round, soft mouthfeel; keep sulfate low to avoid harsh bitterness edge. Mash at 65 °C for a slightly dextrinous wort that supports haze and body. Bittering is light (Magnum at 30 min for clean utilization, no flavor), and nearly all hop character comes from a whirlpool stand plus two staged dry-hops: the first charge at high krausen (peak yeast activity, day 2-3) drives biotransformation of hop terpenes into more aromatic compounds, and the second charge late (after gravity has dropped ~50%, day 6-7) polishes the aroma without pulling excessive vegetative character. Cold-crash and package promptly — NEIPAs oxidize fast.

fermentables.notes: protein + beta-glucan source for haze stability and creamy body

title: Double Soft Cloud Hazy DIPA
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A double hazy IPA — same shape as the single NEIPA but pushed into 8%+ ABV territory with a larger grist, more flaked oats, and a more aggressive three-stage dry-hop schedule. Soft, pillowy, intensely aromatic; designed to show how the NEIPA template scales up.
notes: Same water philosophy as the single — high chloride (~220 ppm), low sulfate (~50 ppm), calcium ~80 ppm — pushed slightly more chloride-forward to balance the higher malt load. Mash at 65.5 °C to leave enough dextrins for body without making the beer cloying. Three dry-hop charges are intentional: the first at high krausen (day 2) drives biotransformation, the second mid-fermentation (day 5) lifts aroma intensity as fermentation winds down, and the third late (day 9, gravity ~75% attenuated) deposits fresh aroma oils that survive packaging. Pitch a larger yeast starter or fresh pack (London Ale III is a low-attenuation strain — plan for ~75% apparent attenuation). Cold-crash, gelatine-fine if needed, package with minimal O2 pickup.

title: Viking's Blood Hibiscus Metheglin
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A traditional metheglin in the 'Viking's Blood' style — wildflower honey fermented clean and dry, then aged on dried hibiscus flowers (with a small amount of dried elderberry and orange peel for depth) in secondary to pull a deep, blood-red colour and a tart, cranberry-like fruit character without harsh tannins.
notes: Hibiscus flowers (and elderberry) are added in SECONDARY, not primary: hot alcohol extraction during primary over-steeps them and pulls bitter tannins and a medicinal edge. A 5-7 day steep in secondary at ~18 °C gives the full colour and flavour without the harshness. Rack off the botanicals on day 7 so the colour and aroma set without continued tannin extraction. Bulk age 8-10 weeks for the hibiscus acidity and honey to round out. The wildflower honey is intentionally light-coloured so the hibiscus red dominates the glass — orange blossom or buckwheat would either compete with or muddy the colour. Stabilize with sulfite + sorbate and lightly backsweeten (~80 g honey dissolved into a sample) so the residual sugar balances the tart hibiscus without re-fermenting in the bottle.

fermentables.notes: light wildflower blend — keeps the colour contribution to the hibiscus

yeasts.notes: clean, neutral fermenter — lets hibiscus lead the aromatics without competing esters

title: Northgate Dry Apple Cider
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A crisp, bone-dry farmhouse cider built from store-bought apple juice with a small dose of concentrate for apple intensity. Fermented fully with a champagne yeast for a clean, neutral, very dry finish — the reference base that every other variation in the set is built on.
notes: Pick juice without added ascorbic acid, potassium sorbate, or sodium benzoate — these preservatives stall yeast. A small concentrate dose at pitch lifts the apple character without bumping the ABV noticeably. EC-1118 ferments bone-dry; if you want a touch of residual sweetness, sub D47 (or stop fermentation early and stabilize). Bottle still or force-carbonate to taste — the natural-carb version uses concentrate priming in a separate recipe.

title: Sunhill Semi-Sweet Apple Cider
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A balanced semi-sweet cider on the same store-bought juice base, fermented dry then back-sweetened with apple juice concentrate and cold-stabilized so the residual sweetness stays put in the bottle. A touch more concentrate at pitch and slightly bumped acid keep the sweetness from reading flabby.
notes: Back-sweetening with concentrate keeps the apple character consistent with the dry base — no sugar-spike that fights the fruit. Stabilize aggressively: potassium metabisulfite first, then potassium sorbate 24 h later, then back-sweeten. Skip the sorbate and the concentrate will re-ferment in the bottle. Bottle still — carbonating would change the perception of sweetness.

title: Riverbend Naturally Carbonated Cider
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A dry apple cider naturally carbonated in the bottle using apple juice concentrate as the priming sugar. The same concentrate that drives bottle conditioning also adds a final dose of apple flavor — so the cider pours with a soft petillance and a rounder apple finish than the force-carb version.
notes: Priming concentrate is NOT a fermentable in the pre-fermentation sense — it sits in additions[] with timing='at bottling' so it doesn't get counted toward OG. Aim for ~0.30 L concentrate per 19 L batch (roughly 2.5 vol CO2); go to 0.40 L for a more aggressive sparkle. Condition 2 weeks at 18-20 °C for reliable carbonation; chill a test bottle to confirm pressure before opening the rest.
styleName: Standard Cider and Perry (Petillant, naturally carbonated)

title: Blackberry Hollow Apple Cider
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A dry apple cider with a deep, jammy blackberry character from whole fresh (or frozen) blackberries in primary. The fruit's natural acidity lets the explicit citric/malic dose drop, and a generous pectic enzyme charge pulls the heavy berry pectin out of the way for a clear, ruby-purple pour.
notes: Freeze-thaw the berries before pitching for max color and flavor extraction — frozen berries break down faster and give a more reliable ferment. Add pectic enzyme with the fruit, not just at pitch: the berry load is heavier than juice alone. Hold the acid additions down; blackberries already contribute significant malic and citric acid, and over-acidifying turns the cider sharp.

title: Cranberry Bog Apple Cider
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A tart, ruby-pink apple cider built around a serious dose of cranberry character. Cranberries are naturally very acidic and contribute plenty of citric acid on their own, so the explicit acid additions are kept low and a small back-sweetening with concentrate rounds the finish without turning it into a sugary cordial.
notes: Use 100% cranberry juice (not cocktail — that's mostly sugar water and apple). The cranberry acid load is high enough that a typical acid blend would over-acidify; the trick is letting the fruit contribute the sharpness and using a touch of back-sweetening to balance. Cold-crash in secondary to drop the cranberry haze — it's tannic and slow to clear on its own.

processSteps.notes: stir in 0.4 L apple juice concentrate to balance cranberry sharpness — final gravity ~1.012, no re-fermentation because of the sorbate

processSteps.name: Bottle
processSteps.notes: bottle still; force-carbonate to ~2.5 vol if you want a petillant cranberry cider

additions.name: Pectic enzyme
additions.purpose: breaks down apple + cranberry pectin for clarity

additions.name: Citric acid
additions.purpose: TA adjustment — kept low; cranberry already contributes significant citric acid

additions.name: Fermaid-O
additions.purpose: yeast nutrient (organic) — bumped to handle the larger juice volume + cranberry tannins

additions.name: Apple juice concentrate (back-sweetening)
additions.purpose: back-sweetening to balance cranberry sharpness (still cider, no priming)

title: Fireside Spiced Apple Cider
author: Recipe Content Curator
description: A dry apple cider steeped with whole cinnamon sticks, cloves, and allspice berries in secondary for a warm-spice holiday profile — no extract, no fake-cinnamon flavorings. The spice steep is short and whole-spice-only so the aromatics come out clean rather than bitter.
notes: Steep whole spices only — ground spice dusts the cider with sediment and pulls harsh tannins. Five to seven days is enough; longer extracts bitter compounds from the cloves. Pull the spices and rack before bottle conditioning. Bottle still — the spice reads strongest without carbonation masking it. Stable without sorbate because the cider ferments fully dry.

fermentables.name: Store-bought apple juice (no preservative)
fermentables.notes: pasteurized, no sorbate / benzoate / ascorbic added

fermentables.name: Apple juice concentrate (flavor boost at pitch)
fermentables.notes: raises the apple intensity so the spice doesn't dominate

yeasts.name: Lalvin EC-1118
yeasts.notes: neutral ester profile so the spices read cleanly

processSteps.name: Campden rest
processSteps.notes: 24 h post-sulfite, before pitch

processSteps.name: Rack to secondary
processSteps.notes: rack off heavy lees so the spice steep starts clean

processSteps.name: Spice steep
processSteps.notes: add cinnamon sticks, whole cloves, allspice berries in a sanitized muslin bag

processSteps.name: Rack off spices
processSteps.notes: pull the spice bag and any settled sediment

processSteps.name: Bulk age
processSteps.notes: short cold age lets the spice aromatics settle into the cider without fading

processSteps.name: Bottle
processSteps.notes: bottle still — do not carbonate; the spice reads best on a flat cider

additions.name: Pectic enzyme
additions.purpose: breaks down apple pectin for clarity

additions.name: Citric acid
additions.purpose: TA adjustment — primary apple acid; lifted slightly to keep the spice from reading heavy

additions.name: Cinnamon sticks (whole)
additions.purpose: spice — primary warm-spice note; whole only, no ground

additions.name: Whole cloves
additions.purpose: spice — small dose for top-note warmth; over-doing turns the cider bitter

additions.name: Whole allspice berries
additions.purpose: spice — rounds out the cinnamon / clove with a peppery-eucalyptus note
